old_claim.note: Guild Certification Seals provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Contract Law supplies the agreement, obligation, pledge, breach, and enforceability framework for written apprentice contracts.

source_shape.source_locator: Fordham reproduces the 1248 Marseilles apprentice agreement with mutual promises, a forfeitable pledge if the agreement is broken, sworn security, pledged goods, and legal renunciations.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source shows apprentice contracts as enforceable written agreements with obligations, breach penalties, and legal language.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Contract law is the framework that makes the apprenticeship text a binding agreement; guild certification seals attest status or quality and are downstream or adjacent, not the direct prerequisite.

ontology_before: Guild Apprentice Contracts depended on Guild Certification Seals, conflating later attestations of training, membership, or quality with the legal agreement that created apprentice obligations.
ontology_after: Guild Apprentice Contracts depend directly on Contract Law for the agreement and enforcement framework, while guild certification seals remain a separate attestation technology.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: guild_apprentice_contracts now depends on contract_law and no longer directly depends on guild_certification_seals.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to post-training guild certificates or seals rather than apprentice agreements.
A stronger source showed the 1248 Marseilles apprenticeship agreement required guild certification seals as the direct enabling technology.
The contract_law node were narrowed away from enforceable agreements, pledges, obligations, and breach consequences.
short_reason: Apprentice contracts need contract law, not certification seals.
